content: @Miguel Ojeda I find myself in need of something like the BTree from alloc. Do I have to roll my own, or is there a plan to enable more components of the alloc crate?

Also I am a bit curious how the licence situation of alloc in relation to the kernel. As far as I can google fu, Apache code is not compatible with GPL2 code. How is this handled, regardless of alloc living in the kernel tree or not?
BTree from alloc is not fallible.
Have you checked the RBTree binding? https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/kernel/rbtree/index.html

Gary Guo said:

Have you checked the RBTree binding? https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/kernel/rbtree/index.html

Thanks, will take a look

Andreas Hindborg said:

Also I am a bit curious how the licence situation of alloc in relation to the kernel. As far as I can google fu, Apache code is not compatible with GPL2 code. How is this handled, regardless of alloc living in the kernel tree or not?

AFAIK Rust is dual licensed under Apache 2.0 OR MIT and MIT should be compatible with GPL2

Andreas Hindborg said:

Miguel Ojeda I find myself in need of something like the BTree from alloc. Do I have to roll my own, or is there a plan to enable more components of the alloc crate?

We can import anything we may find useful from alloc, it is not a problem.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Also I am a bit curious how the licence situation of alloc in relation to the kernel. As far as I can google fu, Apache code is not compatible with GPL2 code. How is this handled, regardless of alloc living in the kernel tree or not?

The kernel allows code under several licenses, not just GPL-2.0. That includes MIT (which is a "preferred" license for the kernel), and Apache-2.0 when dual licensing a preferred one.
In other words, you cannot just use Apache-2.0, but you can use it when it is dual licensed with a compatible-to-GPL2 one.
In particular, for alloc, Linus asked us to be very clear about the licensing back when we discussed it, but otherwise it is fine.

Miguel Ojeda said:

In other words, you cannot just use Apache-2.0, but you can use it when it is dual licensed with a compatible-to-GPL2 one.

Technically speaking, we are not "using" Apache-2.0. We are solely using the MIT license. The Apache-2.0 part of SPDX merely documents that the code is also available in Apache-2.0.
No, "using" comes from the kernel licensing rules. If we wanted to "use" RandomLicense OR MIT, then we could not unless the license gets added, even if MIT is OK on its own.
Regarding licencing, the derived work we get when combining kernel GPL2 code and alloc MIT code will be covered by GPL2, right?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Miguel Ojeda I find myself in need of something like the BTree from alloc. Do I have to roll my own, or is there a plan to enable more components of the alloc crate?

We can import anything we may find useful from alloc, it is not a problem.

Well, I sort of want the BTree, but as Gary said it does not use fallible allocations. Can I start using it and hope for alloc to eventually add fallible methods, or should I find something else?

For now I can use the RBTree. I think an rbtree is not optimal for my use case, and I do not need anything from C side to access the tree. A pure Rust thing would be better.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Regarding licencing, the derived work we get when combining kernel GPL2 code and alloc MIT code will be covered by GPL2, right?

The kernel is "provided under the GPL-2.0", and it is my understanding that that means the whole is indeed covered by GPL-2.0 (which, as far as I know, is what the GPL-2.0 requires when mixing it with compatible-with-it licenses). Is there a specific use case you are worried about?

Andreas Hindborg said:

Can I start using it and hope for alloc to eventually add fallible methods, or should I find something else?

If you mean upstream alloc, hard to say (the original discussion we had with them was that upstream Rust wanted us to use an unmodified alloc, see rust/alloc/README.md for details).

If you mean our own alloc fork, one potential option is to add them ourselves.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Regarding licencing, the derived work we get when combining kernel GPL2 code and alloc MIT code will be covered by GPL2, right?

The kernel is "provided under the GPL-2.0", and it is my understanding that that means the whole is indeed covered by GPL-2.0 (which, as far as I know, is what the GPL-2.0 requires when mixing it with compatible-with-it licenses). Is there a specific use case you are worried about?

I'm just considering my options. I need a fast btree or a trie and I would rather grab something off the shelf than roll my own for now. Most Rust code is MIT/Apache2, so I was wondering about if that kind of code could be included in the kernel. But it seems that BSD and MIT code can be included and the version distributed with the kernel sources will just be GPL2, and that is OK because the clauses added by GPL2 are not conflicting with the original MIT license.
What do you need the btree for?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Can I start using it and hope for alloc to eventually add fallible methods, or should I find something else?

If you mean upstream alloc, hard to say (the original discussion we had with them was that upstream Rust wanted us to use an unmodified alloc, see rust/alloc/README.md for details).

If you mean our own alloc fork, one potential option is to add them ourselves.

So if I went ahead and enabled the btree in our alloc crate and added fallible allocation methods, that would be upstreamable to the kernel? That would mean that we diverge from Rust alloc, and it is not certain that Rust will accept our fallible btree additions.

Alice Ryhl said:

What do you need the btree for?

I need it for memory backing of the null block driver I am working on. I think a btree or a trie will give better performance that an rbtree because of better cache performance.
The key is the address of a request floored to a page and the value is a struct page
Also, I still did not look into enabling LTO and the C backed rbtree is taking a lot of calls to the C side

Andreas Hindborg said:

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Can I start using it and hope for alloc to eventually add fallible methods, or should I find something else?

If you mean upstream alloc, hard to say (the original discussion we had with them was that upstream Rust wanted us to use an unmodified alloc, see rust/alloc/README.md for details).

If you mean our own alloc fork, one potential option is to add them ourselves.

So if I went ahead and enabled the btree in our alloc crate and added fallible allocation methods, that would be upstreamable to the kernel? That would mean that we diverge from Rust alloc, and it is not certain that Rust will accept our fallible btree additions.

Rust's btree impl is pretty complex and has had UB in the past. It also is a bigger hit on compile times than HashMap. If the kernel already has a btree datastructure maybe a wrapper around it would be better?

bjorn3 said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Can I start using it and hope for alloc to eventually add fallible methods, or should I find something else?

If you mean upstream alloc, hard to say (the original discussion we had with them was that upstream Rust wanted us to use an unmodified alloc, see rust/alloc/README.md for details).

If you mean our own alloc fork, one potential option is to add them ourselves.

So if I went ahead and enabled the btree in our alloc crate and added fallible allocation methods, that would be upstreamable to the kernel? That would mean that we diverge from Rust alloc, and it is not certain that Rust will accept our fallible btree additions.

Rust's btree impl is pretty complex and has had UB in the past. It also is a bigger hit on compile times than HashMap. If the kernel already has a btree datastructure maybe a wrapper around it would be better?

They have a trie that is actually used in the C null block driver. But I don't want to call into C on every block request.

Andreas Hindborg said:

So if I went ahead and enabled the btree in our alloc crate and added fallible allocation methods, that would be upstreamable to the kernel? That would mean that we diverge from Rust alloc, and it is not certain that Rust will accept our fallible btree additions.

It would be technically upstreamable, i.e. there is nothing preventing us from doing so, including licensing. I was confused why you asked because we already have alloc in-tree, and it was of course checked that the licensing was OK a long time ago.

Now, whether we should upstream additions or not is a different question. For fallible methods, adding those is within scope, since that is why we added our fork to begin with. If it is other kinds of methods, or adding our GFP flags, etc., that is a different story, because that is likely not upstreamable to Rust, and we need to think about what will happen there (given the recent developments around the other try_* methods and other discussions).

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

So if I went ahead and enabled the btree in our alloc crate and added fallible allocation methods, that would be upstreamable to the kernel? That would mean that we diverge from Rust alloc, and it is not certain that Rust will accept our fallible btree additions.

It would be technically upstreamable, i.e. there is nothing preventing us from doing so, including licensing. I was confused why you asked because we already have alloc in-tree, and it was of course checked that the licensing was OK a long time ago.

Sure, I did not doubt that you did your homework in that regard :) I was just uncertain about the mechanics and the general policy for grabbing MIT licensed code for inclusion in the kernel.

Ah, I see, sorry, I thought you could have been asked by somebody else about a particular licensing issue for e.g. creating a custom kernel with out-of-tree code or similar, and I am always careful about answering those sorts of questions to avoid misunderstandings.
Is there a reason String was not upstreamed or there wasn't a use case for it? I'm wondering if CString is preffered instead.

Ariel Miculas said:

Is there a reason String was not upstreamed or there wasn't a use case for it? I'm wondering if CString is preffered instead.

It seems it is behind its own feature flag, not no_global_oom_handling. Did you try to enable it and see if it compiles? There is no fallible allocation APIs in the upstream String, maybe that is why it was disabled?

No, I didn't try enabling it, I only needed it for printing out some errors and I just avoided it for now. The missing fallible allocation seems like a probable cause, is there any discussion around this topic? Since it seems like a requirement from the kernel side.

Fallible allocations are not supported in the kernel by design, which means we can't support things like the + operator for String and other operations that assume allocation is infallible (at least not with their original APIs).
I assume you meant "Infallible" as the first word, thanks for the explanation
Yes sorry, I meant "infallible" ^^
